title: Nonequilibrium scaling of drag forces in counterdriven fluid mixtures
authors: Jonas Köglmayr, Florian Sammüller, Matthias Schmidt
abstract: We address the effective nonequilibrium drag force field that emerges from the microscopic interparticle interactions in steady states of counterdriven binary fluid mixtures. Using power functional scaling arguments for adaptive Brownian dynamics computer simulation results, we establish quantitatively the crossover between near-equilibrium linear response and far-nonequilibrium square root asymptotics. An algebraic expression captures both limiting cases and remains applicable in the crossover regime. Using simulation results as benchmarks, we verify that a local power functional approximation based on the scaling law reproduces the spatial nonequilibrium structure formation in inhomogenously driven systems. The crossover scenario transcends dynamical density functional theory and it sheds light on general nonequilibrium scaling of driven fluids.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to quantify the nonequilibrium drag force in counterdriven fluid mixtures. Using power functional scaling arguments, the authors derive a universal scaling law that accurately describes the transition between linear and square-root force dependencies. This work advances the understanding of non-equilibrium physics beyond standard density functional theory.
main_problem: To establish a quantitative scaling law describing the effective nonequilibrium drag force field in steady states of counterdriven binary fluid mixtures.
main_result: The study successfully derives a universal power functional scaling form that captures the crossover between near-equilibrium linear response and far-nonequilibrium square root asymptotics.
method: The authors employ power functional scaling arguments applied to adaptive Brownian dynamics computer simulations, extending classical density functional theory concepts.
model_or_system: The system is a binary fluid mixture ($\alpha=1, 2$) subjected to external forces driving the species in opposite directions (counterdriven). The dynamics are modeled using overdamped Brownian dynamics.
key_observables: Partial density profiles ($ho_\alpha(r)$), partial interparticle force density ($F_{\alpha}^{	ext{int}}(r)$), and the drag force characterized by $G^{	ext{int}}(z)$.
important_parameters: The counterflow velocity difference ($v_\Delta$), the crossover velocity ($v_s$), and the effective density ($ho_s$) are key parameters defining the scaling regimes.
main_assumptions: The analysis initially focuses on planar geometry and assumes the applicability of a local power functional approximation for inhomogeneous systems.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a simulation snapshot of the binary mixture under counterdrive. Subsequent figures illustrate the successful fitting of simulation data across various driving strengths using the derived scaling function.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the force balance equation, derives the scaling ansatz for the drag force, validates this ansatz against simulation data across different regimes (near vs. far nonequilibrium), and finally extends the framework to describe spatially inhomogeneous systems using a local functional approximation.
why_interesting: The development of a universal scaling law for non-equilibrium forces in driven classical fluids provides a powerful, generalizable theoretical tool for understanding energy dissipation and transport in complex many-body systems, which has analogues in quantum many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Top panel: Representative simulation snapshot of the binary mixture under counterdrive. The particles of species 1 (dark red spheres) and of species 2 (light orange spheres) are driven in opposite directions along the z-axis by species- dependent external force fields f (1) ext(z) and f (2) ext(z), which are first taken to be spatially uniform and are later considered to be position-dependent on the z-coordinate. The system is on average homogeneous in the lateral x- and y-directions and lengths are scaled with the common particle size σ. Bottom panel: Illustration of the range of simulated systems with spa- tially homogeneous driving, shown across the plane of scaled force...
